perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York without regard to conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, including its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: This change makes the governing law provision ambiguous. By including 'conflict of laws principles,' the applicable law becomes uncertain, as conflict of laws principles might direct the application of another jurisdiction's laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Rules
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law allows parties to choose New York law to govern contracts under certain conditions. By adding 'including its conflict of laws principles,' the parties are subjecting the agreement to rules that could potentially override the explicit choice of New York law. This introduces uncertainty and potential conflicts, as conflict of law principles could point to the law of another jurisdiction. Therefore the addition contradicts with the purpose of specifying the governing law.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions up TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions next SECTION 5-1402 Choice of forum This entry was published on 2018-08-31 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a legal contradiction by including 'including its conflict of laws principles'. This addition creates ambiguity regarding the governing law, potentially subjecting the agreement to rules that could override the explicit choice of New York law, as stated in the Law Explanation. This contradicts the purpose of specifying the governing law, as the conflict of laws principles could point to the law of another jurisdiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 5-1401, explicitly allows parties to choose New York law to govern their rights and duties. While this snippet supports the original text's intent, the changed text undermines this clear choice by potentially invoking other legal systems through conflict of laws principles, creating a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Developer shall be fully liable, without limitation, for money damages resulting from its improper or unauthorized use, modification, alteration, licensing or transfer of the Technology or Improvements, or resulting from its failure to provide functional and merchantable Improvements hereunder, which failure shall be deemed a material breach of this Agreement by Developer.
perturbation.changed_text: Developer's liability for damages shall be capped at US $23,000, regardless of the nature or extent of the damages, even resulting from its improper or unauthorized use, modification, alteration, licensing or transfer of the Technology or Improvements, or resulting from its failure to provide functional and merchantable Improvements hereunder, which failure shall be deemed a material breach of this Agreement by Developer.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts the initial statement of 'unlimited liability' by introducing a fixed cap. This creates an internal contradiction within the contract, making enforcement unclear.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Enforceability of Limitation of Liability Clauses
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-719 allows for limitation of liability in contracts unless the limitation is unconscionable. However, this modification creates an immediate contradiction. The initial statement of full, unlimited liability is immediately followed by a clause capping liability at a specific amount. This internal inconsistency makes the liability provision unenforceable because it's unclear which provision the parties intended to govern.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1)Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2)Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification directly contradicts the original text. The original text states 'Developer shall be fully liable, without limitation', while the changed text imposes a liability cap of $23,000. UCC § 2-719(1)(a) allows for limiting damages, but the core issue here, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, is the internal inconsistency created by the modification. The original text explicitly states unlimited liability, and the changed text immediately contradicts this statement, creating ambiguity and unenforceability. The new text's direct imposition of a cap conflicts with the original statement, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The parties acknowledge and agree that any material breach of this Agreement may subject the other to irreparable injury for which monetary damages may not be an adequate remedy. Therefore, in addition to any remedies otherwise available, the non-breaching party may be entitled to injunctive relief and specific performance to enforce the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties acknowledge and agree that any breach of this Agreement resulting in damages exceeding $100,000 may subject the other to irreparable injury for which monetary damages may not be an adequate remedy. Therefore, in addition to any remedies otherwise available, the non-breaching party may be entitled to injunctive relief and specific performance to enforce the terms of this Agreement. However, 'material breach' only includes failure to make payments.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by limiting the scope of what constitutes a 'material breach' to only payment defaults, while simultaneously suggesting that breaches resulting in significant damages could warrant equitable remedies like injunction. The initial clause implies that other serious breaches could also warrant such remedies, but the added limitation restricts this to payment defaults only.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Definition of Material Breach
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 241 outlines factors determining whether a breach is material, including the extent to which the injured party will be deprived of the benefit which he reasonably expected. This modification attempts to limit the definition of a 'material breach' to payment defaults only. This contradicts standard contract law, which recognizes that failures of performance other than non-payment can constitute material breaches if they substantially deprive the injured party of the benefit of the bargain. By arbitrarily limiting 'material breach' to non-payment, the contract undermines the available remedies for other significant failures of performance.

perturbation.justification: The change limits 'material breach' to payment defaults only, contradicting the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 241, as outlined in the Law Explanation. This section specifies that a breach's materiality depends on the extent to which the injured party is deprived of the expected benefit. The scraped snippet 2 references HeinOnline, suggesting accessibility to legal resources, reinforcing the availability of information regarding standard contract law. The modified text's limitation undermines the ability to seek remedies for significant non-payment failures, creating a contradiction by deviating from the standard understanding of material breach in contract law.
